cataloging_subject.label: The Wars of Alexander: Skeat's 1886 edited selection
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the U-M/General Corpus edition-level selection encoded from Walter W. Skeat's 1886 EETS Extra Series 47 publication. It includes edition title matter, the verse Wars of Alexander represented through overlapping Ashmole and Dublin witness streams, Skeat's editorial paraphrase of the missing ending, and a separate incomplete prose text editorially headed The Story of Alexander. Omitted front and back matter remains represented by page breaks but is not transcribed. The unit is not one manuscript, not one medieval work, and not two sequential halves of one verse text.

subject_statements.label: Travel, monsters, and the marvelous East
Treachery, mortality, and the fall of rulers

summaries.value: Skeat's edition-level selection brings together two distinct Middle English Alexander works: an incomplete alliterative romance printed from overlapping Ashmole and Dublin witness streams, and a separate incomplete prose epitome from the Dublin codex. The encoded selection also retains editorial title matter, Skeat's prose account of the lost verse ending, and his argument before the prose text.

The verse work narrates Alexander's conquests, marvels, kingship, treachery, and death in unrhymed alliterative long lines. Skeat prints Ashmole and Dublin in parallel where they overlap and uses Dublin to supply Ashmole's lacuna; the streams are alternative witnesses, not additive poem segments.

The separate prose Story of Alexander is incomplete at its end and survives in TCD MS 213 after leaves of accounts and miscellanea. Scholarship reports a possible source in Caxton's second edition of the Dicts and Sayings, but direct collation remains outstanding.

extent.description: Two historical works are represented: one verse work and one prose work. The encoding has 40 verse-group containers and 8,509 serialized L elements, but these include overlapping Ashmole and Dublin witness streams and therefore are not an intellectual poem extent. Verse-group and verse-line totals remain null.

content_parts.label: Wars of Alexander (Alexander Fragment C)
content_parts.summary: An anonymous Middle English Alexander romance in unrhymed alliterative verse, incomplete at its end and represented in Skeat by two overlapping manuscript witnesses.

locators.value: pp. 1-276 for the medieval verse; Skeat's ending paraphrase continues through p. 279

notes: The XML contains 27 passus containers, 40 verse-group containers, and 8,509 serialized L elements: 5,688 in the Ashmole stream and 2,821 in the Dublin stream, with 24 parallel-version containers.
The parallel streams are not additive, so those encoding counts are not stored as intellectual verse-group or line extent.
The Ashmole stream combines 2,735 unwrapped L elements with 2,953 L elements inside Ashmole version containers.
Skeat's final conventional number 5,677 and modern catalog numbering remain unreconciled.

content_parts.label: The Story of Alexander
content_parts.summary: A separate, incomplete Middle English prose epitome copied from IE TCD MS 213 and placed after intervening account leaves in that codex.
notes: The XML has six direct narrative P elements; its separate ARGUMENT/P is editorial and excluded from the historical prose work.
Accounts and miscellanea at current ff. 67r-70r separate this prose from the verse witness.
Khalaf reports that scholars consider ff. 70v-72r copied from Caxton's second Dicts and Sayings edition; the relation remains supported/medium pending direct collation.

related_works.title: Historia Alexandri Magni de Preliis, J3a recension
notes: This identifies a textual source tradition, not a known direct physical exemplar.

related_works.title: William Caxton's second edition of The Dicts and Sayings of the Philosophers
notes: The proposed excerpt relationship applies only to the prose Story of Alexander and remains supported/medium pending direct collation and exact-printing verification.

evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the exact repository selection, encoded titles and contents, U-M identifier, witness labels, division boundaries, folio milestones, editorial notes, and separate prose-story boundary.

evidence.citation: APA1967 durable raw-to-pinned XML and page-sequence computation audit.
evidence.summary: Supports exact fixity, two-work cardinality, non-additive parallel witness streams, prose/editorial boundaries, page mapping, and null intellectual verse extent.

evidence.citation: Walter W. Skeat, ed., The Wars of Alexander, EETS Extra Series 47 (London: N. Trübner, 1886).
evidence.locator: Title page; introduction pp. ix-xxiv; verse pp. 1-276; ending paraphrase pp. 276-279; Story pp. 279-283.
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the edition title, publication, editorial responsibilities, independent work boundaries, witness method, and editorial ending and argument.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, APA1967.0001.001.
evidence.summary: Confirms the current U-M item identity, discovery information, encoded contents, and public access.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Wars of Alexander (Alexander Fragment C).
evidence.summary: Supports the preferred part title, c.1450 date, tentative Northumbria region, alliterative form, NIMEV identifier, Historia J3a source tradition, and witness loci.

evidence.citation: Middle English Compendium, bibliography BIB3575, Wars of Alexander.
evidence.summary: Confirms NIMEV *3947.3, DIMEV 6306, the two witnesses, Skeat's edition, and the preferred-manuscript and Dublin-lacuna arrangement.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Ashmole 44, catalog description by Charlotte Ross (2024).
evidence.summary: Supports the Ashmole witness identity, single-text contents at ff. 1r-97v, date, northern context, physical description, lacuna, and numbering cautions.

evidence.citation: APA1967 durable Dublin witness foliation and content crosswalk audit.
evidence.summary: Supports current verse ff. 27r-66v, intervening accounts at ff. 67r-70r, prose ff. 70v-72r, and Skeat's restarted prose foliation.

evidence.citation: Omar Khalaf, Patronage, Print and the Education of the Gentry, in English Literature and the Gentry in Late Medieval England (2021).
evidence.summary: Secondary support for the proposed Caxton-source relation of TCD MS 213 ff. 70v-72r; direct collation remains outstanding.

open_questions.question: Can Skeat's conventional 5,677 endpoint, the XML's 5,688 Ashmole L nodes, and the Bodleian catalog's 5,803/approximately-6,300 account be crosswalked without conflating editorial systems?
next_steps: Collate the three numbering systems against passus, witness, and lacuna boundaries before supplying an intellectual line extent.

open_questions.question: Can Skeat's Dublin line ranges be reconciled with the 2024 Bodleian catalog's modern ranges?
next_steps: Create a page-and-line concordance from a complete Dublin reproduction and the modern edition.

open_questions.question: What is the current stable item-level Trinity catalog URL and complete physical description for IE TCD MS 213?
next_steps: Ask Trinity Research Collections for the current catalog record and shelfmark history.

open_questions.question: Does direct collation confirm that The Story of Alexander derives from Caxton's second Dicts and Sayings edition, and which exact printing is intended?
next_steps: Collate TCD ff. 70v-72r against the second-edition print witness and Blake 1991.

open_questions.question: Will Bodleian provide a complete-color or complete-reference facsimile of MS Ashmole 44, and under what repository/publication license and quote?
next_steps: Request a whole-codex quote and written redistribution terms; identify target ff. 1r-97v.

open_questions.question: Will TCD provide a complete-color facsimile of IE TCD MS 213, and does an item-specific CC BY 4.0 statement apply to the delivered files?
next_steps: Request the complete codex, identify verse ff. 27r-66v and prose ff. 70v-72r, and obtain item-specific reuse terms.

open_questions.question: What exact preferred authority identity should replace Skeat's source-limited Mr. Hessels, and can George Parker be normalized beyond Skeat's printed name?
next_steps: Search authority files and EETS correspondence without expanding either display name from inference alone.

notes: Edition title matter at TIFF 1-2 is paratext and contributes no historical work count.
Skeat's editorial paraphrase of the missing ending at printed pp. 276-279, TIFF 298-301, and 14 P elements nested in the final verse L/NOTE1 is not counted as medieval verse.
Skeat's ARGUMENT/P before The Story of Alexander at printed p. 279 is editorial introduction and is excluded from the historical prose work.
Omitted front and back matter survives only as encoding placeholders, chiefly front PB references to TIFF 3-21 and back PB references to TIFF 306-506.
U-M's 2006 digital-production date is recorded on the encoding and facsimile entities in the paired lineage manifest because the work-metadata date vocabulary has no digital-production type.
Witness copying dates, dialects, locations, and image terms are not promoted to the abstract historical works.
